title: And How Does That Make You Feel?

chapters.title: Therapy has become the new gym
chapters.text:
The line "I am seeing a therapist" no longer surprises anyone, but sixty or seventy years ago people kept it to themselves. Back then, anyone who could manage alone was treated as fine.

Society has shifted a long way. The old script of "keep calm and carry on" has given way to "you should feel proud of yourself for asking for help."

Still, the road ahead is long. With searches like "how to manage anxiety" climbing on Google, plenty of people hesitate to try individual therapy. Their reasons run from a fear of opening up to a poor match with a specialist.

Neither oversharing nor bottling everything up helps. Therapy may be the answer.

One thing is clear. There is a real need for more context and openness about therapy. Joshua Fletcher, a well trained psychotherapist and anxiety specialist, gives a glimpse into the therapeutic process and breaks down complex subjects such as panic attacks and obsessive compulsive disorder.

If you want to clear the air about therapy for yourself and the people you care about, read on.

chapters.title: Anxiety is here to stay
chapters.text:
Anxiety is often called the disease of the 21st century, but it has been with humans for far longer than any written word. Early humans survived because of anxiety. Not today’s version, but the raw instinct that later grew into it.

Nature gave humans a threat response system that registers danger and forces the body to react by fighting, fleeing, or freezing. A specific brain center, the amygdala, acts as the chief of that fight or flight response.

That threat response was an advantage in the past. Being alert and anxious in the right way let humans spot predators from a distance and gave them time to decide what to do. Fighting a lion is rarely smart, so hiding in a cave was a common choice.

Here is the tricky part. Unlike other brain centers, the amygdala did not evolve. It is like keeping an old pair of pajamas even though you can now afford a better set. The world is safer, yet evolution may still know better.

Because a threat is largely subjective, whatever we see as scary can light up the amygdala. Where humans once feared lions and bears, they now fear rejection or gossip. Modern anxiety is not exaggerated, just driven by a different kind of threat.

In the end, getting rid of anxiety is a fool’s errand. It is part of the system that keeps us safe. We need those fear responses to avoid dark streets and spot danger.

The internet is full of mental health advice, but it is not tuned to your specific situation and issues.

We can still re rank our threat hierarchy and trim unnecessary anxiety. Some things we fear are not really scary. If you worry that new coworkers will not like you, that does not mean something is broken. It may mean you are holding on to a false belief about yourself, and the therapy room is a great place to change that.

chapters.title: When your own body turns against you
chapters.text:
One of the most unpleasant side effects of anxiety is a panic attack. A panic attack arrives suddenly, with a rush of adrenaline, chest pain, high blood pressure, shortness of breath, and an all consuming wave of terror.

It can feel like an invisible force takes over. Let us take the mystery out of panic attacks by looking at why they happen.

Joshua Fletcher calls each of us an inner stress jug that holds our capacity for pressure and anxiety. Some people are more resilient than others, yet no one is fully safe from panic attacks. Once the stress jug overflows, the body sounds the alarm.

No stress is trivial. Any stress you do not handle can endanger your well being.

Picture a teenager going through a tough year who has to give a presentation to the whole school. Just before stepping on stage, a panic attack hits, and it is not about puberty or fear of public speaking. Every stressor piled up at once, and the body gave way.

The hardest part comes later. After riding out that wave of panic, the brain saves the memory and labels it as a threat. From that point on, every trigger looks bigger, because the person does not know which one will set off another bout. They get stuck in a loop of fearing fear itself.

There is still hope. Exposure Therapy is a strategy where a person, under a specialist’s care, faces the triggers in order to relabel them in the brain.

In the case of the teenager, once they have given a speech to ten or fifteen people, the amygdala registers that it is not so dangerous. There is a long road ahead, but at least the body does not shut down at the sight of an audience.

If you or someone you love struggles with panic attacks, know that it is not a terminal condition, and there is a safe way out.

chapters.title: Your thoughts are not you
chapters.text:
As we keep moving through the world of anxiety, the next stop is Obsessive Compulsive Disorder, or OCD. This condition is often misunderstood, so let us set the record straight.

OCD is an anxiety disorder in which someone gets stuck in a loop of repetitive thoughts, called obsessions, and repetitive behaviors, called compulsions. Picture your brain as a radio that gets stuck on the same station instead of switching on purpose. That alone would be annoying, but OCD also brings intense worry and the feeling that you have to act on every anxious impulse.

Many people mistake OCD for a love of order. If someone’s shirts are sorted by color in the closet, that does not mean they have OCD. They just like to organize.

OCD often travels with intrusive thoughts, which are upsetting ideas or images that slip into the mind. Common kinds include violent thoughts about hurting yourself or others, including loved ones. Sexual thoughts about inappropriate acts, such as those involving minors, family members, or animals. A driving need to keep everything in perfect order to prevent something terrible. And a nervous urge to check again and again that the door is locked or the iron is off.

People with this disorder do not act on impulses that harm others. The thoughts harm only them.

So is there a fix? Every case differs, but Exposure and Response Prevention, or ERP, is a strong and popular tool for this. For someone dealing with intrusive thoughts, ERP might include looking at photos of the people the thoughts center on. They let the thoughts run without trying to cancel them, and the amygdala rewires itself, so troubling thoughts slowly fade.

Instead of fighting intrusive thoughts, try accepting them without judgment. The more you resist, the louder they get.

One vital point about OCD is that thoughts do not define a person.

According to the National Institute of Mental Health, around 1.2 percent of adults in the United States have OCD.

chapters.title: Dealing with a heavy blanket of gloom
chapters.text:
Try picturing waking up alone under a heavy blanket that is cold and sticky rather than cozy and puffy. That image sits close to what depression can feel like. Clinical depression does not just visit for one gloomy day. It can paint weeks and months of life in shadow.

Depression pulls the mind into a loop of worst case scenarios, self doubt, and a hollow sense that nothing really matters. A person under its weight feels numb, cut off, and empty inside.

The condition also carries physical symptoms, including deep tiredness, pounding headaches, broken sleep, and shifts in appetite. That is where self criticism often creeps in. When the body stops working the way it should, it is easy to slip into harsh thoughts. Instead of turning on yourself, reach out to a friend or a trained professional.

Judging yourself when you are low never helps, as Joshua Fletcher has noted.

Fletcher suggests a simple image for anyone learning about depression. Picture a lens. There is real depth to that single word.

A person in love sees the world through rose tinted glasses. Depression colors everything through a gray tinted lens. Even though depressive feelings and thoughts feel real and convincing, remember that the lens is the problem, not the world behind it. The view is not the objective truth, and there is always a way to clean the lens.

Depression draws people into isolation, yet healing rarely happens alone. The therapy room can help.

Depression can come from hormonal imbalances, unhelpful personal beliefs, guilt, anxiety disorders, or painful life events. A therapist’s job is to trace where the depression began and to help clients move through the healing process.

Depression is not a sign of weakness. It is part of being human in a complex and imperfect world.

chapters.title: Why bother with therapy
chapters.text:
Even though therapy is a buzzword these days, plenty of misconceptions still float around. Let us clear up a few.

Some people try therapy and say, "It did not work." Compare therapy to sports for a moment. After one cycling class, can you say you have tried sports? You might learn that cardio is not for you, but that does not mean you have tried every sport, since there are others you might enjoy, like pilates or tennis.

Therapy works the same way. Therapists specialize in one or a few techniques and cannot cover the whole field of mental health. Some focus on gestalt work, while others dig into childhood wounds. The fit has to be right.

Try different approaches until you find a specialist who matches what you need.

Of course, there can be ethical problems. If a therapist turns sessions around to talk about themselves, fails to give you a sense of care, or breaks confidentiality, you are free to end the relationship. No one owes a stranger a long commitment if the connection is off.

Some people think, "I can vent to a friend over a few cocktails for less than a therapist’s fee." That may be fair, but even the best friend cannot offer professional advice.

Opening up to a friend can lift the moment, while sessions with a therapist can help you grow into a steadier version of yourself over time.

Therapists train to stay in your frame of reference. They step into your feelings and use empathy. Friends cannot do that, even with the best of intentions. Friends tend to stay in their own bubbles and react to how you make them feel. A therapist focuses on how you feel.

Good therapy is a safe space where all of you is welcome, as Joshua Fletcher has put it.

Professional help is not only for people with mental disorders. Anyone can work through small problems and learn to handle emotions in the therapy room. It is never too late to get to know yourself.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Therapy is not lofty talk between a professional in a three piece suit and a client, as movies sometimes show. It can be messy, full of tears and sweat, but it is worth the trouble. There is real beauty in the healing, the restoration, and the discovery of yourself.

Even with the challenge of finding a good therapist, great things happen when there is a natural match. When a client finds the courage to open up and drop the guard, the therapist meets them halfway with the tools to help sort everything out.

A few habits can help you get the most from the work.

Check your stress jug every day to keep it from overflowing.

Look at what causes you to feel anxious and decide whether the threat is real or imagined.

Learn more about therapy techniques so you can find the right match with a specialist.

Talk about mental health with family and friends to raise awareness.
